text: Here is a video demonstration of advanced robotic surgery for early cervical cancer.
The robotic arms perform a type II radical hysterectomy, followed by bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy and dissection of bilateral pelvic lymph nodes.
The surgeon uses an endoscopic stapler to close the vaginal cuff prior to performing a colpotomy.
This is the case of a forty eight year old multi pyrus lady who was evaluated elsewhere for heavy menstrual bleeding.
She underwent colposcopy guided biopsy which was reported as CIN-III cervical intraepithelial neoplasia.
Leap procedure was done, the histopathological examination of which was suggestive of a well-differentiated squamous cell carcinoma.
However, there were no remarks on the size of the lesion, margin status or lymphovascular space invasion.
And these blogs were not available for review at our center.
There was no obvious lesion on the cervix on examination as well as on MRI.
Patient opted for a definitive surgery over the option of observation and follow-up.
The robotic-assisted hysterectomy is performed, showcasing the use of robotic instruments for minimally invasive surgical removal of the uterus.
The final histopathological examination report of the hysterectomy specimen revealed no intraepithelial neoplasia or invasive carcinoma and the lymph nodes were also negative for malignancy.
The surgeon initiates the procedure by incising the peritoneum between the round ligament and the infundibular pelvic ligament on the right side.
The surgeon exposes the external and internal iliac vessels along with the ureter, revealing these anatomical structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon performs ureterolysis, carefully dissecting and freeing the ureter from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon dissects the ureter along its course from the infundibulopelvic ligament to its entry into the bladder, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon creates the pararectal space and identifies the uterine artery within the lateral pararectal space using laparoscopic visualization.
The surgeon clips the uterine artery at the point where it crosses over the ureter, ensuring clear visualization of both structures.
The surgeon cauterizes the uterine artery using bipolar energy and then cuts it, following its identification and exposure in the lateral pararectal space.
The surgeon creates a window in the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, forming the infundibulopelvic (IP) pedicle.
The surgeon clips, cauterizes, and cuts the infundibular pelvic ligament, completing the separation of this structure from surrounding tissues.
A window is created in the anterior leaf of the broad ligament, and the round ligament is cauterized and cut near the lateral pelvic wall.
The surgeon continues dissecting the anterior leaf of the broad ligament, progressing towards the junction with the uterovascular fold of the peritoneum.
The surgeon dissects the bladder and pushes it downward, positioning it below the upper one-third of the vagina.

The surgeon repeats the dissection and cauterization steps on the opposite side, creating windows in the anterior and posterior leaves of the broad ligament and managing the infundibular pelvic ligament.
The surgeon opens the left retroperitoneal space, revealing the iliac vessels and ureter, which are clearly identified within the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the ureter and creates the left pararectal space, revealing the anatomical structures within the retroperitoneal area.
The surgeon clips, cauterizes, and cuts the left uterine artery at the point where it crosses over the left ureter, using surgical instruments to ensure precise separation.
The surgeon clips, cauterizes, and cuts the left infundibulopelvic ligament, completing the separation of this structure.

The surgeon dissects and lateralizes the ureter, then cuts the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, extending the incision up to the pouch of Douglas. The rectovaginal space is created, and the rectum is pushed downwards.
The surgeon cauterizes and cuts the left round ligament near the lateral pelvic wall.
The surgeon dissects the anterior cervico-vesical ligament, also known as the ureteric tunnel, and lateralizes the ureter, moving it away from the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a 60mm angulated endoscopic stapler to seal the vaginal cuff in preparation for colpotomy.
The surgeon carefully fires the endoscopic stapler while ensuring protection of the bladder and both ureters, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon opens the distal sealed edge of the vagina and delivers the specimen out through the vaginal opening.
The surgeon performs bilateral pelvic lymph node dissection, carefully removing lymphatic tissue from both sides of the pelvic region.
The surgeon removes fibrofatty tissue surrounding the external and internal iliac vessels, maintaining a parallel orientation to these vessels during the dissection.
The surgeon sutures the vaginal vault using 3-0 barbed sutures in a continuous manner, securing the tissue after colpotomy.
This concludes the video demonstration of robotic assisted type II radical hysterectomy with the use of endoscopic stapler for vaginal closure.
Thank you.
